title: practical english usage - Part 65
content:
to do. There is little difference between may and might in this case.

because there is nothing better, nothing more interesting or nothing more useful This structure is used informally to suggest that one should do something 3 may/might           as   well You may be my boss, but that doesn't mean you're better than me.

are definitely true, not just possible.

Note that in this structure, may and might can be used to talk about things that She might have had a lovely voice when she was younger, but...

It may be a comfortable car, but it uses a lot of fuel.

clever, he ...     Although he’ s clever, he .. .) He may be clever, but he hasn't got much  common sense. (= Even if he's main argument. They  are often followed by but.

even if. to say that something is true, but that this makes no difference to the May (and sometimes might) can be used in a discussion rather like although or 2 may/might          ...  but grammar •  73   strong obligation: must, will That carpet should be cleaned. (= It would be a good idea.) That carpet must  be cleaned. (= It is absolutely necessary.) Should can be used as a weaker form of must (►  76). Compare: 8  must      and   should The doctor said that I had to / would have to stop smoking.

Obligation can also be reported with had to and would have to.

The doctor said that I must  stop smoking.

Must can be used after a past reporting verb as if it were a past tense.

7 indirect      speech:     He  said     I must      stop.

All staff will submit weekly progress reports.

orders.

Will can be used in affirmative structures to give impersonal, military-type Will you be quiet!

Will you send me the bill, please? Come this way, will you?

We   use will you to tell people what to do.

6 orders     with    will:   Will     you     be   quiet!

Nadia isn ’t in her office. She must  have gone home. (= It seems certain that to go home.) Nadia isn’t in her office. She had to go home. (= ɪt was necessary for her the past (►  69). Compare: participle. The  structure with must have... is used to express certainty about Note the difference between had to + infinitive and must  have + past My parents had to work very hard to build up their business.

I had to cycle three miles to school when I was a child.

the past. Had to is used to talk about obligation that existed in the past.

behaviour - for example through orders or advice - and one cannot do this in speech - see below). This is because must is used mainly to influence people’ s Must is not normally used to talk about past obligation (except in indirect 5 past    necessity       and   obligation:       They      had     to   work      very     hard.

visa ... (                                  visa-togo hr Scotland.) or     ou You don’ t need to get a visa to go to Scotland,  Y  don’ t have to get a not   ou work... (     -Y  mu stn't. . .-ifyeu-don't want t&) or     ou You needn’ t work tomorrow if you don’t want to. (BrE)     Y  don’ t have to needn’ t (BrE), don’t need to (►  532) or don’ t have to.

Mustn’t is not used to say that things are unnecessary. This idea is expressed by 4 mustn't        and   needn't       /don't     have      to You mustn ’ t/can’ t open this parcel until Christmas Day.

The government mustn ’ t/can’ t expect people to work for no money.

not to do things. Can’t is also possible.

We   use must not / mustn ’t to say that it is wrong to do things, or to tell people 3 negatives:      You     mustn't/can't          . ..

Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  74  strong obligation: have (got) to I can't come to work tomorrow morning because I've got to see the dentist.

- I've got bad toothache. I must  make an appointment with the dentist.

This is a lovely party, but we've got to go home because of the children.

- This is a terrible party. We really must  go home.

I've got to stop smoking. (Doctor’ s orders.) - I must  stop smoking. (I want to.) than ... she must often go to New York.) My wife's an interpreter: she often has to go to New York. (More natural - We must  go to New York soon - we haven't seen your mother since Christmas.
